metadata.title: Claude, Dreaming.
metadata.topic: Talk analysis: Lamis Mukta's 'Learning while you sleep: Beyond memory to dreaming' — context engineering as an intelligence multiplier, the year of simple memory primitives, four production principles, the in-band ceiling, and dreaming as the out-of-band curation loop
metadata.purpose: deep-explainer talk-analysis episode — analyzes the argument of an external conference talk (AI Native DevCon London, June 2026) in the Teardown register
metadata.audience: practitioners and makers following the channel's Claude workflows
metadata.source: Lamis Mukta (Anthropic, Applied AI), 'Learning while you sleep: Beyond memory to dreaming', AI Native DevCon, London, June 2026 — https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tTcxVv8HHNw — full timestamped transcript on disk as SOURCE.md in this folder
acts: II — The Year of Simple Things
metadata.notes: Logo bug @HumanitariansAI lower-right on every beat (stated once here, per LOGO LAW). TIER 3 SPEAKER RULE: Lamis Mukta is a real named person — NO stills and NO generated imagery of her, ever; she is credited by name in B00 narration and on the B41 credit card only. All vox stills are Tier 1 generic (bookshelf, card catalog, filing cabinets, bedside table, exam hall / marked papers, exam clock), shot.source ai. Every production-results claim (better accuracy, lower cost, one-shotting) is narrated AS the speaker's reported production experience, never as independently verified fact. DOUBLE-CHECK LAW sharpened per episode brief: no product call-to-action in narration — her own Q&A notes she could not make product CTAs; the mechanism is the story. The B41 SOURCES card may note the talk's own Q&A pointer for viewers. On-screen quotes are VERBATIM from the transcript, cited small: Source: Lamis Mukta, AI Native DevCon 2026. One terracotta accent moment per beat.

beats.narration_text: Merhaba — this is Kore, in for Humanitarians AI. Today's source is a conference talk: Lamis Mukta, of Anthropic's applied AI team, at AI Native DevCon in London — on why agent memory shouldn't stop at remembering. Her claim: give your fleet a second, slower loop — and let your agents dream. Let's tear down what that means.
beats.new_visual_element: Cold open — composer ask, answered

show.event: ClaudeComposerAsk: serif greeting 'Merhaba, Kore' with terracotta spark; the ask types itself
running indicator 'reading the talk…'
output lands, line by line: the two-loop answer

props.command: My agents forget everything overnight. This talk from AI Native DevCon says they should be dreaming instead. What does that actually mean?
output: Fast: agents write small memories in-band, during the session.
Slow: an out-of-band batch job reads the whole fleet's transcripts,
finds the repeated failures, and proposes what everyone should remember.

beats.narration_text: Act one. Her opening move is a claim about compounding — and it decides whether any of this is worth your time.
beats.new_visual_element: Act card I

show.event: serif act card 'The Multiplier', terracotta period

beats.narration_text: New models keep getting smarter. Her point: in your org, that intelligence doesn't compound by itself — the model ships knowing nothing about your codebase, your users, your taste. Context is orthogonal to intelligence, and no model release fixes orthogonal.
beats.new_visual_element: Two curves — raw intelligence vs deployed usefulness

show.event: curve one draws rising step by step: 'raw model intelligence', release ticks on the axis
curve two draws nearly flat beneath it: 'what it does in your org' — the gap between them shades
the word 'orthogonal' lands on the gap; terracotta arrow shows the gap does not close as releases tick on

production_viz.label: the gap no release closes
production_viz.mechanic: rising intelligence curve vs flat org-usefulness curve; gap shaded and held constant across release ticks
production_viz.note: schematic — no invented numbers; axes unlabeled by value

beats.narration_text: So context engineering isn't a workaround better models will delete. Her exact words: it multiplies the intelligence, even as models get smarter. That's the thesis — a multiplier survives every upgrade. The rest of the talk is her defending it.
beats.new_visual_element: Quote card — the multiplier claim, verbatim

show.event: quote card types in, verbatim: 'this over time has the effect of multiplying the intelligence even as models get smarter'
terracotta underline draws beneath 'multiplying' on the spoken word
small citation lands: 'Source: Lamis Mukta, AI Native DevCon 2026'

production_viz.label: the thesis, verbatim
production_viz.mechanic: serif quote card; underline keyed to the spoken word; small citation

beats.narration_text: The symptoms are familiar. Agents that don't know their way around the codebase. Agents that ignore your preferences. And the expensive one — the same mistake, every session, forever. That's the disease this talk is treating.
beats.new_visual_element: Symptom chips — the three familiar failures

show.event: three chips light in narration order: 'lost in the codebase' · 'ignores your preferences' · 'same mistake, every session'
the third chip pulses and duplicates across a row of session frames — the repeat made visible
terracotta strike over the row: 'no learning between runs'

props.note: third chip replicates across session frames; the repeat is the point

beats.act: II — The Year of Simple Things
beats.narration_text: Act two. One year of context engineering, compressed. Anthropic's stated rule: do the simple thing that works. Watch how simple it stays.
beats.new_visual_element: Act card II

show.event: serif act card 'The Year of Simple Things', terracotta period

beats.act: II — The Year of Simple Things
beats.narration_text: Stop one: CLAUDE.md. A plain markdown file — your codebase's layout, your conventions, your preferences — injected at the start of every session. Her verdict: unreasonably effective. A text file steered the agent better than anything cleverer. Hold that thought.
beats.new_visual_element: OndaCodeBlock — a CLAUDE.md file, real markdown

show.event: OndaCodeBlock renders a short CLAUDE.md: repo layout note, a convention line, a preference line
terracotta highlight bar sweeps the preference line as 'preferences' is spoken
quote chip lands: 'kind of unreasonably effective' — cited 'Source: Lamis Mukta, AI Native DevCon 2026'

props.code:
# CLAUDE.md

## Codebase
- services live in src/services, one folder per domain
- tests mirror src paths under tests/

## Conventions
- run make lint before every commit

## Preferences
- explain tradeoffs before writing code
props.note: quote chip overlay: 'kind of unreasonably effective' (transcript 4:01)

beats.act: II — The Year of Simple Things
beats.narration_text: The failure mode is baked in: everything injects up front, so the file only grows. Watch the context window — the more preferences you save, the less room remains for the task. The mechanism that steers the agent slowly crowds it out.
beats.new_visual_element: The growing file eats the context window

show.event: a context-window bar; a small CLAUDE.md block sits at its head, the rest labeled 'task'
preference lines append one by one; the file block swells; the task region visibly shrinks
terracotta bracket on the squeezed task region: 'the steering crowds out the work'

production_viz.label: injected up front, forever
production_viz.mechanic: front-loaded file block grows inside a fixed context bar; task region shrinks in proportion
production_viz.note: mechanism from transcript 4:29–4:42; no invented token counts

beats.act: II — The Year of Simple Things
beats.narration_text: Stop two: memory tools, in-band. The agent itself decides, mid-session, when to read, write, or update its own memory. Anthropic's finding — autonomy works. Agents manage memory fine without a human-designed schema. Trust arrives earlier than you'd expect in this story.
beats.new_visual_element: In-band memory ops on a session timeline

show.event: a session timeline runs left to right; the agent chip advances along it
memory ops fire at the agent's own choice: READ pulls a card up, WRITE drops a card down, UPDATE swaps one
terracotta tag on the decision points: 'the agent decides when'

props.note: in-band = within the session's own context; ops keyed to narration order

beats.act: II — The Year of Simple Things
beats.narration_text: Stop three: skills — and her analogy deserves a rebuild. You own a wall of books. You have not read them all into your head. You just know the spines.
beats.new_visual_element: VOX run R1 (1/2): the bookshelf in lamplight, tight on spines
beats.prompt: Tier 1. A wall bookshelf in warm lamplight at night, rows of worn book spines, one slim dictionary spine subtly distinct among them; quiet study mood, no people. One plate serves B09+B10 (tight on spines, then pull wide). See SHOPPING.md.

show.event: treated plate, tight on the lamplit spines; slow lateral drift along the shelf
serif label: 'you just know the spines' — terracotta hairline underline

beats.act: II — The Year of Simple Things
beats.narration_text: Someone starts speaking French. You scan the shelf, spot the French dictionary, and pull that one book — exactly when the conversation demands it. Seven years of French class, skipped.
beats.new_visual_element: VOX run R1 (2/2): pull wide — the whole shelf, one spine ringed

show.event: camera eases OUT from the handoff frame — the whole wall of books resolves
terracotta ring draws around the one dictionary spine on 'that one book'
serif label: 'pulled when the conversation demands it'

beats.act: II — The Year of Simple Things
beats.narration_text: That's progressive disclosure, mechanized. The agent reads only the front matter — a few sentences per skill, like spines on a shelf. The full body loads on demand. Deep detail, near-zero standing cost. Watch the context meter: it barely moves until the pull.
beats.new_visual_element: Progressive disclosure — the shelf of skills, rebuilt as mechanism

show.event: a shelf of skill cards showing spines only — each spine is a front-matter title, a couple of sentences wide
a context meter at the frame's edge ticks up a sliver as the spines register — near zero
an incoming request tagged 'French' slides in; the matching skill card pulls off the shelf and unfolds its full body
the context meter jumps ONLY now; terracotta marks the jump: 'loaded on demand'

props.note: shelf-of-spines adaptation: chips render as book spines with front-matter titles; one unfolds; context meter overlay keyed to the pull

beats.act: II — The Year of Simple Things
beats.narration_text: And the landing point of the whole year: memory as a plain file system. Markdown for reading. Bash and grep for search — progressive disclosure with forty-year-old tools. Her summary line, verbatim: markdown is great for reading memories.
beats.new_visual_element: VOX: the card catalog — memory as a searchable file system
beats.prompt: Tier 1. A vintage library card catalog in warm side light, one drawer pulled open with index cards visible, brass label frames on the drawers; no people. See SHOPPING.md.

show.event: treated plate; slow push toward the open drawer
three serif labels land in turn: 'markdown' · 'bash' · 'grep'
quote strip, verbatim: 'Markdown is great for reading memories.' — cited 'Source: Lamis Mukta, AI Native DevCon 2026'; terracotta period

beats.narration_text: Act three. Every neat idea so far was proven on one agent, one session. Production is where it meets a fleet.
beats.new_visual_element: Act card III

show.event: serif act card 'Meet Production', terracotta period

beats.narration_text: Problem one: two agents open the same memory file, both write, one overwrites the other. The classic lost update. Nothing in markdown-plus-autonomy prevents it — the simple thing that works has no answer for simultaneous writers.
beats.new_visual_element: The lost update — two writers, one file

show.event: one memory file card center; two agent arrows approach from left and right
both write; agent A's edit lands, then agent B's edit stamps OVER it — A's lines visibly vanish
terracotta X over the vanished edit: 'lost update'

production_viz.label: two writers, one file
production_viz.mechanic: second write overwrites the first; the lost lines fade visibly

beats.narration_text: Problem two is worse. One agent hits an error, decides the lesson belongs in the org-wide context — and gets it wrong. Every agent reads that file. One bad write scales to the entire fleet. Her word for it: disastrous.
beats.new_visual_element: One bad write fans out to the fleet

show.event: one agent node writes a line into a central 'org-wide context' document
read-lines fan out from the document to a whole grid of agent nodes; the bad line tints each one as it arrives
terracotta flare on the fan-out: 'one write, every reader'

props.note: the contamination propagates along read edges; transcript 9:38–9:50

beats.narration_text: Then the quieter rot. Humans and agents editing the same memories — who changed what? Facts that were true last quarter and aren't now. And the hostile case: prompt injection writing lies into the store on purpose.
beats.new_visual_element: VOX: the filing cabinets — shared, stale, and spoofable
beats.prompt: Tier 1. A row of grey office filing cabinets under cold fluorescent light, several drawers ajar, folders slightly disordered, a few loose papers on top; no people. See SHOPPING.md.

show.event: treated plate; slow drift along the cabinet row
serif labels land in turn: 'who changed what?' · 'stale'
quote strip, verbatim: 'maliciously injected' — cited 'Source: Lamis Mukta, AI Native DevCon 2026'; terracotta underline

beats.narration_text: Watch what staleness does: agents keep reading the dead fact and acting on it, confidently. Memory has no expiry date and no immune system by default. Guardrails are not polish here — they are the difference between a memory and a liability.
beats.new_visual_element: The dead fact keeps being read

show.event: a memory card in the store greys out — the world changed, the card didn't
agent after agent reads it anyway; each read spawns a confident action chip downstream
a second card slides in looking identical — injected, indistinguishable
terracotta line under both: 'no expiry, no immune system'

production_viz.label: stale reads, confident acts
production_viz.mechanic: greyed card still feeds reads; injected card renders visually identical to legitimate ones

beats.narration_text: Act four. Her four production principles — and notice, as they land, which discipline they are borrowed from.
beats.new_visual_element: Act card IV

show.event: serif act card 'Four Principles', terracotta period

beats.narration_text: Principle one: versioning. Every memory update records which session produced it, which transcript justified it, who wrote it — agent or human — and rolls back when it turns out wrong. Provenance plus undo. You've met this before: version control.
beats.new_visual_element: The version stack — provenance and rollback

show.event: a memory file's version stack builds upward: v1, v2, v3 — each version carries chips: session id · transcript · author
v3 flags bad; a rollback arrow arcs from v3 back to v2, and v2 restores as current
terracotta label on the stack: 'version control, rediscovered'

props.note: each layer carries provenance chips; rollback arrow animates on the spoken word; transcript 10:37–11:14

beats.narration_text: Principle two: concurrency. Before writing, the agent hashes the file. Drafts its edit. Hashes again. A mismatch means someone else wrote in the meantime — so it re-reads, re-drafts, retries the commit. Say the quiet part: this is optimistic locking, straight out of databases.
beats.new_visual_element: Hash-check-before-commit, animated as a loop

show.event: the loop draws as stations: HASH → DRAFT → HASH AGAIN → COMPARE
run one: hashes match, COMMIT stamps green-less — ink check on cream
run two: a rival write slips in mid-draft; hashes MISMATCH; the path loops back to re-read and re-draft
terracotta label lands: 'optimistic locking' — the B14 collision replays and is caught this time

production_viz.label: hash, draft, hash, compare
production_viz.mechanic: two passes through the loop: clean commit, then mismatch-and-retry; names the pattern optimistic locking
production_viz.note: mechanism from transcript 11:19–11:57; 'ripples the memory' in the transcript read as re-reads — logged in FACTCHECK

beats.narration_text: The audience saw it too. From the floor: at what point are we reinventing databases from first principles? Her answer, on record — yes, deliberately. They let agents freewheel, watched which primitives held, and there's no need to reinvent the wheel.
beats.new_visual_element: Q and A card — the databases admission, verbatim

show.event: Q card types in, verbatim: 'at what point are we reinventing databases from first principles again?'
A card answers beneath, verbatim excerpt: 'we are going back to the software engineering principles that we've seen work well in the past'
closing line lands with terracotta period: 'there's no need to reinvent the wheel' — cited 'Source: Lamis Mukta, AI Native DevCon 2026, Q&A'

production_viz.label: the admission, on record
production_viz.mechanic: stacked Q/A quote cards, typed in sequence, citation small

beats.narration_text: Principle three: permissions. The carefully curated org-wide knowledge at the top — read-only to agents. The agent's own scratchpad at the bottom — read-write. Team layers between. One agent can no longer poison the fleet: it can't touch what everyone reads.
beats.new_visual_element: The permission stack — read-only summit, read-write floor

show.event: layer stack builds: 'org-wide knowledge' on top with an RO badge, 'team / cross-section' mid with scoped badges, 'agent scratchpad' bottom with RW
the Act-three bad write replays — an agent's write arrow hits the top layer and BOUNCES off the RO badge
terracotta glow on the scratchpad: 'write here, not there'

props.note: the B15 poison write replays and is blocked; transcript 12:09–13:01

beats.narration_text: Principle four: portability. All that curation is an asset — so it lives behind a clean API, reachable from every surface where your agents run. Memory locked inside one product gets re-curated somewhere else. Treat it as infrastructure.
beats.new_visual_element: The memory hub — one store, many surfaces

show.event: a memory-store hub draws center; an 'API' ring wraps it
spokes connect outward one by one: chat surface, coding surface, pipeline runner, internal tool
a walled-off copy of the store appears in a product box, then fades — the anti-pattern dismissed
terracotta ring pulses on the API ring: 'infrastructure, not a feature'

production_viz.label: one store, every surface
production_viz.mechanic: hub-and-spokes; the siloed anti-pattern appears and fades

beats.narration_text: What she reports from production: second attempts land better, because the failure notes exist. Fewer tokens per task, because agents one-shot what they've seen before. No public numbers — but the claim is consistent: memory pays for itself in retries you never run.
beats.new_visual_element: Reported results — chips, explicitly tagged as speaker-reported

show.event: three result chips land in narration order: 'better accuracy, second time' · 'fewer tokens' · 'one-shots the task'
a persistent tag stamps across all three: 'speaker-reported — AI Native DevCon 2026' — no invented numbers appear
terracotta underline on 'retries you never run'

props.note: hard rule: every chip carries the speaker-reported tag; transcript 14:07–14:47

beats.narration_text: Act five. So build all of that, and you are done? No. In-band memory has a ceiling, and the ceiling is structural.
beats.new_visual_element: Act card V

show.event: serif act card 'The In-Band Ceiling', terracotta period

beats.narration_text: Ceiling one: the split. Mid-task, every token spent curating memory for future runs is a token not spent on the task you asked for. Her phrase — a very difficult optimization problem. Two masters, one budget.
beats.new_visual_element: One budget, two masters — the split-focus slider

show.event: one token-budget bar; a divider splits it: 'the task now' left, 'future selves' right
the divider tugs back and forth — more curation means the task region shrinks, and vice versa; neither side ever wins
quote chip, verbatim: 'a very difficult optimization problem' — cited small; terracotta on the divider

production_viz.label: task now vs future selves
production_viz.mechanic: single budget bar with a contested divider; tension never resolves

beats.narration_text: Ceiling two: visibility. Every session opens a fresh context window — the agent that made yesterday's mistake isn't in the room today. Across a fleet, no agent sees another's failures. Cross-session patterns are invisible from inside a session, by construction.
beats.new_visual_element: The session grid — same mistake, opaque walls

show.event: a grid of session windows tiles the frame; each opens blank — 'fresh context' flashes on each birth
the same red X error appears in window after window; opaque walls render between every pair
terracotta trace draws the pattern ACROSS the grid — visible only to us, the outside view — label: 'no one inside can see this'

props.note: each chip is a mini context window; the repeated failure renders identically in each; the cross-grid trace is the beat's one accent; transcript 16:35–17:02

beats.narration_text: Her move follows: if learning can't happen well during the work, schedule it for when nothing else is running. A separate process. Its own budget. Whole-fleet visibility. She calls it dreaming — and the name is more literal than it sounds.
beats.new_visual_element: VOX: the bedside table at night — out-of-band, literally
beats.prompt: Tier 1. A bedside table at night: lamp switched off, an alarm clock glowing softly, moonlight from a window, a closed notebook and pen beside the clock; calm, no people. See SHOPPING.md.

show.event: treated plate; very slow push toward the glowing clock
serif label: 'while nothing else is running'
terracotta underline draws beneath 'dreaming' as the word is spoken

beats.narration_text: Act six. Dreaming — the out-of-band loop. She explains it with a school, so we will too.
beats.new_visual_element: Act card VI

show.event: serif act card 'Dreaming', terracotta period

beats.narration_text: Picture the marking table. Every student's paper, gathered in one place — a view no student ever gets. The teacher reads across all of them, and patterns surface that no single exam could show.
beats.new_visual_element: VOX run R2 (1/2): marked papers, tight — the red pen view
beats.prompt: Tier 1. A school exam hall: rows of empty desks receding; the foreground desk stacked with marked exam papers, red pen corrections visible; a large plain clock on the far wall. One plate serves B30+B31 (tight on the marked papers, then pull wide to the hall). See SHOPPING.md.

show.event: treated plate, tight on the stack of marked papers; slow drift across the red pen marks
serif label: 'a view no student gets' — terracotta hairline

beats.narration_text: That's the head teacher's advantage, and she names both halves: dedicated capacity for helping others learn, and visibility over the whole hall. We run schools this way for good reason. Dreaming gives a fleet the same two things.
beats.new_visual_element: VOX run R2 (2/2): pull wide — the whole exam hall

show.event: camera eases OUT from the handoff frame — the whole hall of desks resolves, the clock on the far wall
serif labels land: 'dedicated capacity' · 'whole-fleet visibility'
terracotta underline under 'for good reason'

beats.narration_text: First example: every geography student flunks the same question. The head teacher checks the curriculum — the topic was never taught. Fix the curriculum, not the students. In agent terms: the memory store lacked a fact the whole fleet needed. Add it once.
beats.new_visual_element: The missing curriculum topic — pattern, cause, one fix

show.event: a row of paper cards each stamps the same red X on the same question number
the curriculum list draws beside them; a gap yawns where one topic should be — the Xs draw connecting lines to the gap
the missing topic slots in ONCE; the next row of papers renders clean
terracotta label: 'fix the curriculum, not the students'

production_viz.label: one gap, every failure
production_viz.mechanic: repeated identical failures trace back to a single curriculum gap; one insertion clears the next cohort
production_viz.note: her analogy, transcript 19:55–20:30; mapped to memory-store gap in narration

beats.narration_text: Second example: every maths answer wrong the same way — radians where degrees belonged. The fix is one instruction about the calculator's configuration, not smarter students. For agents: the transcripts show a tool call failing repeatedly, so patch the tool configuration.
beats.new_visual_element: VOX: the exam-hall clock — radians struck, degrees drawn on
beats.prompt: Tier 1. A green-rimmed institutional exam-hall wall clock, close up on a plain wall, slightly off-center with clean margin space around it for draw-on annotation; no people. See SHOPPING.md.

show.event: treated plate holds on the green clock face
terracotta draw-on: 'RAD' handwritten beside the clock, then struck through; 'DEG' draws beneath it — keyed to the spoken words
serif label: 'fix the tool, not the agent'

beats.narration_text: So what does that look like as machinery? We asked Claude to draw the pipeline exactly as she describes it.
beats.new_visual_element: ASK micro-beat: draw the dreaming pipeline

show.event: ClaudeComposerAsk micro-beat, spark line 'The Pipeline, Verbatim' — command already typed, running indicator on

props.command: Draw the dreaming pipeline exactly as the talk describes it: a memory store, a batch of session transcripts including tool-call metadata, an orchestrator deploying a fleet of subagents, and proposed memory changes — with supporting transcripts and prevalence stats — that a human accepts or rejects.

beats.narration_text: Here's the batch job. Inputs: the memory store, plus a window of session transcripts — and not just the dialogue. The tool calls and their metadata come too; that's where failures actually live. An orchestrator fans out subagents to read everything, then collects the findings.
beats.new_visual_element: RESULT: the dreaming pipeline — inputs, orchestrator, fleet

show.event: two input stacks slide in: 'memory store' and 'session transcripts'; a second layer fans out of the transcripts — 'tool calls + metadata' — highlighted as its own stream
the orchestrator node lights; subagent nodes fan out beneath it, one per transcript bundle, reading in parallel
findings flow back up to the orchestrator; terracotta pulse on the tool-metadata stream: 'where failures live'

props.note: tool-call metadata drawn as a distinct stream per transcript 21:01–21:23 and 22:05–22:13

beats.narration_text: The output is not a silent rewrite. Each proposed change arrives with receipts: the supporting transcripts where the pattern occurred, and prevalence stats on how often. A human accepts or rejects each one. The dream is reviewed before it becomes memory.
beats.new_visual_element: The proposal card — diff, evidence, prevalence, human gate

show.event: a proposal card assembles: a memory diff up top — one line struck, one line added
evidence chips clip on beneath: three supporting-transcript stubs; a prevalence counter ticks up beside them
ACCEPT and REJECT buttons render at the card's foot; a cursor hovers
ACCEPT clicks — terracotta confirm — and ONLY then does the diff merge into the store behind the card

props.note: proposal-card adaptation: diff + supporting-transcript chips + prevalence counter + accept/reject; merge is gated on the click; transcript 23:12–23:42

beats.narration_text: Act seven. Two loops — and the design tell that says where this whole field is heading.
beats.new_visual_element: Act card VII

show.event: serif act card 'Two Loops', terracotta period

beats.narration_text: The loops run in parallel. Fast loop, in-band: small memory writes during the session, visible by the next run. Slow loop, out-of-band: batch curation with fleet-wide sight. Neither replaces the other — the fast loop learns quickly, the slow loop learns correctly.
beats.new_visual_element: Two loops, one store — different speeds, drawn live

show.event: the memory store draws center; a small tight loop spins fast around it, tagged 'in-band — every session'
a much larger loop draws around both, rotating slowly, tagged 'out-of-band — batch'; both run simultaneously
terracotta labels land on each: 'learns quickly' · 'learns correctly'

production_viz.label: fast writes, slow curation
production_viz.mechanic: two concentric loops at visibly different angular speeds around one store; both alive at once

beats.narration_text: The tell, from her own Q and A. First: let agents freewheel in markdown, committing whatever they want. Then: watch which primitives survive contact. Now — her words — codifying that in the harness. Deliberately hardening the winners into deterministic code. Maturity, not retreat.
beats.new_visual_element: Freewheel, observe, harden — the three-phase tell

show.event: phase one card: loose markdown scribbles committing freely — 'freewheel'
phase two card: some scribbles fade, a few solidify into crisp primitives — 'watch what survives'
phase three card: the survivors snap into a rigid harness frame — verbatim strip lands: 'codifying that in the harness' with small citation
terracotta frame around the harness: 'the winners, hardened'

props.note: quote strip verbatim from Q&A, transcript 30:33 answer; cited 'Source: Lamis Mukta, AI Native DevCon 2026, Q&A'

beats.narration_text: The trade she names head-on: dreaming looks expensive — a whole batch job spending tokens on no user task. Her reported experience runs the other way: effective memory one-shots tasks, retries vanish, total cost goes down. In her accounting, the dream is the cheap part.
beats.new_visual_element: The cost intuition, inverted — as she reports it

show.event: the naive ledger draws: task cost bar, plus a new 'dreaming batch' bar stacked on top — total looks worse
the reported ledger draws beside it: retry bars shrink away run by run as memory takes effect; the total drops below the naive one
a persistent tag stamps the reported ledger: 'speaker-reported'; terracotta arrow on the falling total

production_viz.label: the dream is the cheap part
production_viz.mechanic: two ledgers: naive stacked-cost intuition vs reported retries-vanish accounting; no invented numbers, bars schematic
production_viz.note: presented AS her reported experience, transcript 24:48–25:02

beats.narration_text: Full credit — this is Lamis Mukta's talk: Anthropic's applied AI team, AI Native DevCon, London. Everything on screen was rebuilt from her transcript. Link and details on the card and in the description — the full talk is worth your half hour.
beats.new_visual_element: Sources and credit card — the speaker, named

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Context multiplies intelligence — raw capability alone doesn't compound in your org. A year of design landed on the simplest thing: markdown files, searched. Production hardened that into software engineering — versions, hash checks, permissions, portability — deliberately. In-band memory hits a ceiling: split focus, no sight across sessions. Dreaming is the slow loop — out-of-band, its own budget, fleet-wide visibility, human-gated. And she reports cost runs downhill.
beats.new_visual_element: VERDICT recap

show.event: ClaudeVerdictArtifact: title 'Memory writes. Dreaming curates.' — five bare lines land with the voice

props.title: Memory writes. Dreaming curates.
artifactLines: Context multiplies intelligence; raw capability doesn't compound alone.
A year of design landed on the simplest thing: markdown, searched.
Production re-converged on software engineering — deliberately.
In-band memory cannot see across sessions.
Dreaming: out-of-band curation, evidence attached, human-gated.

beats.narration_text: Your turn — run a dream tonight, manually. Here's the prompt: Here are transcripts from my last few Claude sessions. Act as my out-of-band reviewer — run a dreaming pass. One: find the mistakes, corrections, and preferences that repeat across sessions. Two: for each pattern, quote the supporting evidence verbatim and count how many sessions it appears in. Three: propose specific edits to my CLAUDE.md or memory files — one edit per pattern, additions and deletions both. I will accept or reject each proposal. Paste your real transcripts underneath, and watch for two things: does every proposal carry quoted evidence — no receipts, no edit — and does it propose any deletions? A reviewer that only adds is missing the stale half of the job.
beats.new_visual_element: YOUR TURN — the manual dreaming pass

show.event: ClaudeComposerAsk, greeting 'Your turn.', the full prompt types in as Kore reads it aloud
on the discussion lines, two terracotta checkmarks annotate the composer margin: 'evidence quoted?' · 'any deletions?'

props.command: Here are transcripts from my last few Claude sessions [paste them below]. Act as my out-of-band reviewer — run a dreaming pass. (1) Find the mistakes, corrections, and preferences that repeat across sessions. (2) For each pattern, quote the supporting evidence verbatim and count how many sessions it appears in. (3) Propose specific edits to my CLAUDE.md or memory files — one edit per pattern, additions and deletions both. I will accept or reject each proposal.
